speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to just hold this. November the 20th, 1995. Survivor, Magda Hedges Mittler. Interviewer, Lucy Chipkin, Sydney, New South Wales, Australia. Language is English. I'm Lucy Chipkin. Today is the 20th of November, 1995. I'm interviewing Magda Hedges in Sydney, New South Wales, Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Magda Hedges.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hedges is H-E-D-G-E-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mittler, M-I-T-T-L-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Margaret I am using in Australia. Because when we came, I found it's very hard for people to say Magda. It was an unusual name then, 44 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you every known by any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. I mean, yes, first names, yes. Because my husband born Heimann, H-E-I-M-A-N-N. Then he chose a name for escaping from Hungary. It was Hegyeshalmi, which is a very hard name to spell. You want me to spell it as well?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: H-E-G-Y-E-S-H-A-L-M-I. So we arrived to this country with that name. And soon I was ever asking for changing the names. First we got Heges, actually, which is a H-E-G-E-S. After naturalization, we could be Hedges and a proper English name. So there are lots of name changes in my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: 10Th of May, 1921.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age?

speaker: interviewer
text: And the city and country of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Budapest, Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Magda, tell me about growing up in Budapest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Growing up. Well, in the first 12 years of my life, was quite pleasant and easy. Except that my father had the occupation, which was a night occupation mostly, because he was in the restaurant business. And he came home at night and got up mid-day, about. And I remember as sort of afraid of him. Because we never had much in common. We never had time to talk. My mother was the one who was-- not really spoiling me, but gave me everything what was possible. But when I was 12 years old, my father left my mother, after 30 years of marriage. Because I had three sisters, one brother. And I was born after 10 years of my sister, of my last sister actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were four daughters or three daughters?

speaker: interviewee
text: With me, three daughters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Including you, it's three daughters, so you had two sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two sisters. I mean, three sisters, but one of them was a twin, died right after birth. So I didn't even know her. Just heard of her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And an older brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the brother was the oldest. And then my sister who lives in Australia, she's 88 now. And my other sister went after the war to England. And, before Yom Kippur, she was rushing home to do the cooking, and it was a rainy day. And she had a car accident. And she was 12 years in a Jewish Home, invalid, before she died.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's the story of the family. And my childhood was not very pleasant in a way. Because after, when I was 12 years old and my father left us, my mother was struggling. And she also died when I was 16 years old, in the hospital. And then I had to leave school at 16 and start to work. Let's go a little further back. Let's go to the first 12 years, before your father had left your mother. What part of Budapest did your family live in?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in the seventh district, which is Pest, Pest called Pest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a Jewish area?

speaker: interviewee
text: I could say yes, yes. Not a ghetto area, but a Jewish area. Lots of Jewish people live there. Sixth, seventh district was always-- the fifth district was for the higher class Jews and would have, as always, for the Gentiles, mostly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live? In an apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other Jewish families in the apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, not when I was in my younger age. That was only a two-bedroom flat, and all the children were home. So it wasn't very comfortable. But as a young person, I didn't even know notice. I had quite a pleasant childhood, as I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What school did you go to at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: First I went to [? Schott's ?] College, which was very close by. And it was quite a-- well-off children there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a private school or a government school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Private school. And in the high school, I went to a Jewish school. After 14 years old I was. No, no, no, sorry. Finishing off in Hungary when you are 10, then you go to high school. At 14 years old, this high school finished. Not the high school certificate, just the-- what is before high school certificate actually, when you are 14?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a school leaving certificate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leaving certificate, not leaving certificate. Was it-- I really can't translate it in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it in Hungarian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Finished-- it's like start secondary school, you know, secondary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Going in high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were at the first school, the [? Schott's ?] College, did you have any experience of anti-Semitism at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all. Not at all, because we were 52 pupils in one class. And out of this, 48 were Jewish. So it was no question about it. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they all girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was a girls school. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you attend any Jewish education classes?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] religious hours when they teach you but on the other hand, we had to say every morning the Christan prayer. So I learned both.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have religious instruction at the school itself? Jewish instruction?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just one hour a week. Yes, and Friday night we could go to shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any [NON-ENGLISH] or any other religious instruction apart from school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not then. I only had it after, when I went to the Jewish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your family's religious affiliation.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was very religious, but very, very plain woman. And she really didn't know the implication of the school where I went to. She thought that so many Jewish girls, and that is a better class than actually, you know, than the public school. So she was satisfied that I'm going there, until a few people advised her to take me out, because I don't get the right religious tuition. So that's why I got into the Jewish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say that your mother was religious, did you have an observant home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, I should say. Without wearing a wig or anything, but she kept everything-- like she was born in the country, she kept all the habits of the Jewish holidays, all the rituals. And my father wasn't. I wouldn't say that he was overly religious. But my mother always been.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about any of those rituals that she observed?

speaker: interviewee
text: The rituals? Well, let's say that in [NON-ENGLISH] time, she had to burn the bread on a wooden spoon. And the chicken had to be turned around her head, I don't know how many times. It was properly done, like in the country. I really don't exactly remember everything what she done. But she was a kosher household, she kept a kosher house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any family members around you, apart from your immediate family? Did you have grandparents or aunts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My mother's mother was living with us, until she died. And she was also religious and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Esther [? Kohn. ?] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And actually, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] she must have been Shwarcz, because my mother is Shwarcz. [? Kohn ?] was her maiden name, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother have any brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had an aunt in Donaufeld Var, which is on the Danube. More than a village, bigger than a village.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that where your mother came from?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's where my mother came from. And the sister was living there, and I spend usually the summer holidays, school holidays, there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Staying with your aunt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Staying with my aunt, and she's had two children. And one of them, when he was up to Budapest for high school, came up and living with us when my mother had lodgers, when she lived alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: I am a bit mixed up, don't I? No? Don't I mix up the stories? Is it understandable? Absolutely understandable.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big was the village that your mother came from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I really couldn't tell you the size of it. I mean, they were small villages in Hungary, where there were just 1,000 people. This has, I think it must have about 10,000 people in the village.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was a Jewish community within the village?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were Jewish, but I didn't remember. There were a few families I know, who were Jewish there. And they had on the marketplace, they had a shop, like a haberdashery, sort of shops. And on Saturday mornings, there was a market, open market. And they had the table on the market. And that's how they sold, like old timer Jewish people. They lived on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were a child you used to see this when you went to visit your aunt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, sure, sure. I enjoyed this very much. I take part. I was on the square and running around with the other children and helping them to pack, when somebody bought something. I never had the luxuries at a young age, but my mother was trying everything. I went to learn ballet. I went to learn piano for six years. When I was six years old and finished at 12. And she even bought, with her spare money, a piano for me, so I can play at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your mother survive financially after your father left?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was cooking for different people, like take-away food, what you call here, take-away. And we had a couple of lodgers. And one of them was my auntie from Donaufeld Var's son. Her son came over to go to school in Budapest. And he lived with us and paid for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zoltan Markstein. It's a hard name.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I know that when he married-- he never went to war. I know that. He was quite clever, and he managed not to go. And he married a girl from Czechoslovakia. They lived for a while in Hungary. Then they went to Vienna, and came to Australia. But they couldn't settle very well in Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had two children and went to America, where my other auntie lived, who is also my mother's sister. But she went before the First World War to America, with her husband. And they didn't have any children. So, can I mix up a little bit with the Australian business? Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, she was the one. I was the first relative to come out behind the Iron Curtain whom she could get in touch with, because I didn't even know her. She was about 80 years old, when I was 30 and I came here. And I wrote to her, because we were in need of money. And I also wanted to know who is alive in the family. And her husband was dead by then. And she send me money-- not money, ticket for going by boat to America to see her. And I was staying with her for six weeks. And it was a return trip. And that's how I met her first time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was your mother's oldest sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My mother's older sister. And my mother had a brother, who died before the war. He's had cancer. So I think she had another brother in [NON-ENGLISH] which is also a little bit bigger than Donaufeld Var And she was the-- I mean, sorry, he was the highlight of the family, because he was working for the council in their village. And they find him very intellectual this time. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of child were you? What sorts of things did you like doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a kind of tomboy, always liked sports, always running around. And I always had just one friend. I never had a big crowd around me. In the elementary school, I had one very close friend. But then we were day and night together, let's say. And then in high school also, I had a very close friend. I was quite well with the other kids but only kept just one real friend. I must have been a bit-- not very outgoing, come to think of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sorts of sports did you enjoy?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- first of all swimming, which I still continue. I never learned swimming, but I take part in a swimming club competition. Because when my sister was learning swimming, she's had a coach. And I was next to her, and I learned what the coach said. And so I never had actually a paid coach. And I worked myself up to quite a good standard in swimming. And I was skiing, and rowing, and bicycle, and mountaineering. So everything that was possible. And I joined a club, which was a Jewish club. And we did all these kinds of sports there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the club called?

speaker: interviewee
text: Its, if I say it in English, it's VAC, which was [HUNGARIAN] Club. But [HUNGARIAN] is fencing. It was fencing and athletic club, if I translate it from Hungarian. But I never did fencing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was a Jewish club?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jewish club, yes. And there, I have a lot of friends. Still, some people who lives here in Australia, remember me from the club. We were together. And my very best friend in the school, is also-- was here in Australia. Unfortunately, she died two days ago. Her funeral is tomorrow. She was a very well-known name, Nellie Wida. Maybe you heard of her because she's had a very high class dress shop in Double Bay.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were friends with her since--

speaker: interviewee
text: Since I was 10 years old. Yes. With the whole family, we were very close. She was also very poor. And we had our little enjoyments, whatever we could afford. But when I left school at 16, I had to go to work. And then I had to keep myself, because my mother wasn't alive by then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you living with when your mother died?

speaker: interviewee
text: My older sister who is coming, was here in Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many years older is she than you?

speaker: interviewer
text: So was she married at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was divorced by then. And she was living in a flat. And I was with her. And that's how I survived, somehow. But when she married second time, I went to live with them as well. Because I was still not married.

speaker: interviewer
text: What work did you do when you left school?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, I was for a few months, just modeling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, well, that friend of mine, whom I just mentioned, Nellie, she was a model in a very good couturier house. And she told me to go in with her to modeling. But my mother was very much against it. Because-- I can't call a lady, but a girl from a good family hasn't had-- she thought hasn't had much chance to find a nice husband, if she's in the modeling business. Because they have do a lot of things, which she didn't agree.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had to leave it. And I went to-- after high school, I went two years to an academy, where I learned [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] shorthand and typing. And then I got an official.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that office job, in an advertising agency, I kept until the war, when I had to go. And they collected me, the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your boss in the advertising agency.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a thorough gentleman, without children. And he was a cello player, a very gentle soul. And the wife was also a piano teacher. And they took me aside with their daughter. Because my circumstances were like that. They knew that I am practically by myself in the world, beside my sister. And they were the ones who lifted me to higher level education in music and opera. And they took me places where I learned a lot. So I owed them a lot. And I always called them a second parent.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was Arnold Kisch. And the wife was Caroline. And the wife wasn't Jewish, he was Jewish. And he's had five brothers, six boys all together. And when the war came, the wife was hiding whoever was left, including her husband. But when I went to the working camp, she was the one who looked me up and went through a lot of people, until he found me. Because I was sort of lost in the wilderness.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was the person who found you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she was. She notified my husband, who wasn't my husband then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did her husband survives the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: He survived the war, yes. He survived the war. But she was so much Jewish looking that I was always worried that something happening to her. Because she's had the red hair and the hooked nose. And it was so funny because the husband didn't look Jewish, but she did. And she had to do everything for everybody, you know, to cook and buy the food. Besides, as I told you, that she couldn't agree that I was lost. She went after every channel to find me. And then she found me in the hospital, where I was taken from the working camp, which is-- I'm going ahead of time, sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after you left school and you lived with your sister, you began working in the advertising agency doing secretarial work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did a lot of different work. Because only another girl was working there, it wasn't a big office. And I was there longer than her. And, of course, my boss was working. And I did a lot, what is connected with correspondence and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know at this point about Hitler coming to power in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we heard of it. But we never believed that he will come to Hungary. Because as far as I know, Hungary was the last one. The only problem was, before the Germans came in, the Hungarian Arrow, what they called himself-- Arrow--

speaker: interviewer
text: Arrow Cross.

speaker: interviewee
text: Arrow Cross people, they were so cruel. Worse than-- we thought it's much worse then the Germans could ever be. So we had them before the Germans came. Because they were followers of the German trend then. And we only knew about the news that when they occupied Danzig and the corridor and-- of course, we heard on the radio everything. But actually, we never heard for a long time that they take away the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't know that they were taking away?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did know but not in the beginning, not In the beginning. Of course, when you listened to radio, the radio, Hungarian radio said whatever they wanted to say. But if we had a chance to listen to British or American radio, then we learned a little bit more of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll just hold it there.
